titleStmt.title: 142. Telegram From the Embassy in Cambodia to the to the Department of State

div.head: 142. Telegram From the Embassy in Cambodia to the Department of State Source: Department of State, Central Files, POL CAMB–US. Secret; Immediate. Passed to the White House.
div.p: 561. Eyes only for Hilsman. Am giving following explanation here for postponement my return to Washington on consultation until early January:

Following exchange between Dept and Embassy regarding problems withdrawal USAID and MAAG, Dept asked me to postpone my return until early January in order that I might be here to deal with problems which might arise in connection withdrawal and remain until majority personnel of two Missions depart. Am sending note to FonOff,
as follow up to previous note announcing my departure Dec 23, saying I will be proceeding to Washington on consultation in early January following withdrawal most of USAID and MAAG personnel.

Would be helpful know what Dept has in mind in connection with any possible negotiations with RKG. I should like to repeat and reemphasize views set forth my tel 546. Document 141. There is no real hope improvement US-Cambodian relations so long as Sihanouk is unquestioned arbiter this country’s destinies and policies, and I do not see how US overtures to Sihanouk, which he might interpret as surrender to his high-pressure tactics, could lead to basic change his attitude or policies. I am convinced best line for us to continue to follow is that set forth in Embtel 455,See footnote 7, Document 138. with which Dept concurred in its Deptel 305,Dated December 6. (Department of State, Central Files, AID (US) CAMB) that we should not agree to continue any elements US aid program unless RKG makes clear its desire that we do so and submit request in writing. Sihanouk is clearly using our friendly gestures to avoid complete rupture as means strengthening before masses image of his infallibility and belittling US overtures as yielding to Sihanouk’s clever and superior tactics. Influential and well-connected Cambodians have suddenly begun to display new confidence and renewed friendliness toward Americans which may well presage turn in tide. These are Cambodians most directly affected by Sihanouk’s rejection US aid and his new economic measures, and they have for some [time?] avoided contact with us. This is only straw in wind but these Cambodians in excellent position to be aware of changes sometime before they have been translated into concrete action.

Economic and financial facts of life may be coming home to roost sooner than we anticipated. Flight of leading banker reported in Saigon tel 1203 to DeptDated December 22. (Ibid., POL 30–1 CAMB) and recent Phnom Penh Presse editorial criticism Sihanouk’s new economic policies (whom Sihanouk immediately denounced publicly in strongest terms as bordering on treason) offer other indications that all is not sweetness and light for Sihanouk.

National Congress meeting on December 29 should provide forum for Sihanouk to show in what direction events in Cambodia are moving and will move in immediate future. I think it would be extremely unwise for us to take steps at this stage which would represent appeasement of
Sihanouk or departure from our agreed upon course of action (Embtel 455 and Deptel 305). To surrender now would only whet Sihanouk’s appetite and likely lead to even more outrageous abuses of US in future, thus postponing evil day as it were, whatever that day and Sihanouk might bring to us. Even more importantly, it would undermine position and courage Cambodian moderate elements who are our only hope for long pull.
